puzzles.headline: A wall that divided a city for 28 years finally comes down

monday: This happened during the final chapter of a decades-long geopolitical standoff.
The leader of the Soviet Union had recently introduced sweeping reforms.
tuesday: This occurred in a year of significant economic upheaval.
A cultural milestone in this same year changed music forever.
wednesday: This was a major geopolitical event in the latter half of the 20th century.
The year saw significant changes in European governance.
clues.thursday: This occurred in the second half of the 20th century.
clues.friday: A major Cold War milestone fell in this year.
clues.saturday: The world was watching something else unfold that day.

puzzles.context_card: For 28 years, Checkpoint Charlie was the defining symbol of the Cold War. On November 9th, 1989, a confused bureaucrat announced that East Germans could cross freely — and thousands flooded through. No one had planned the announcement. The wall fell because the system was already crumbling.

puzzles.event_formal: First powered airplane flight by the Wright Brothers
puzzles.headline: Two brothers from Ohio change transportation forever in a field in North Carolina

monday: This happened in the early years of a new century.
The inventors were two brothers who ran a bicycle shop.
tuesday: The event took place on a beach known for its constant winds.
The flight lasted less than a minute.
wednesday: This occurred in the first decade of the 20th century.
The event happened in North America.
clues.thursday: This was an American invention that changed the world.
clues.friday: The first flight lasted under 60 seconds.

puzzles.context_card: The Wright Brothers made four flights at Kitty Hawk on December 17th, 1903. The first lasted just 12 seconds and covered 120 feet. By the end of that day, they had flown a total of 59 seconds. Orville would later say that the sensation of flight never fully wore off on him — it just kept getting better.

puzzles.event_formal: First human landing on the Moon
puzzles.headline: A man takes one small step that changes humanity's place in the universe

monday: This happened in the year humans first walked on another world.
The mission was part of the space race between two superpowers.
tuesday: The event happened in the summer and was watched by millions worldwide.
The commander was from Ohio.
wednesday: This was a major achievement of the 1960s.
The year also saw Woodstock and a famous Woodstock music festival.
clues.thursday: The first words spoken on the surface were about this being one small step.
clues.friday: This was in the 1960s.
clues.saturday: This changed how we see our place in the universe.

puzzles.context_card: Neil Armstrong's first steps on the Moon were watched by an estimated 600 million people — one-fifth of the world's population. The lunar module was called Eagle. Armstrong had only 30 seconds of fuel remaining when he found a landing spot. He called it 'magnificent desolation.'

puzzles.event_formal: RMS Titanic sinks on maiden voyage
puzzles.headline: The largest ship afloat sinks on its first voyage, taking over 1,500 lives

monday: This was a maritime disaster that shocked the world.
The ship was called 'unsinkable' before its maiden voyage.
tuesday: The ship was bound for New York when it struck an iceberg.
Fewer lifeboats were aboard than regulations required.
wednesday: This was an Edwardian-era tragedy.
A famous film about it was released in 1997.
clues.thursday: The ship was called the largest in the world at the time.
clues.friday: This was in the early 20th century.

puzzles.context_card: The Titanic was carrying some of the world's wealthiest people and hundreds of immigrants seeking a new life in America. It struck the iceberg at 11:40 PM and sank in under three hours. The nearby RMS Carpathia rescued 705 survivors. The disaster led to major changes in maritime safety regulations.

puzzles.headline: The assassination of an archduke triggers a war that reshapes the world

monday: A political assassination in the Balkans sparked this conflict.
The war involved most of the great powers of the era.
tuesday: The war introduced trench warfare and new weapons of destruction.
It was triggered by an event in Sarajevo.
wednesday: This began in the decade before the Great Depression.
The conflict lasted four years and involved four empires.
clues.thursday: A Balkan assassination triggered this war.
clues.friday: This happened in the 20th century.

puzzles.context_card: The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo on June 28, 1914, set off a chain reaction of alliance obligations. What was supposed to be a short war became a four-year conflict that killed 17 million people and destroyed four empires: the German, Austro-Hungarian, Ottoman, and Russian empires.

puzzles.headline: A day that changed America and the world forever

monday: This happened in the early 21st century.
Terrorists hijacked four commercial airplanes.
tuesday: Two planes struck the tallest buildings in New York City.
This led to the creation of the Department of Homeland Security.
wednesday: This happened on a Tuesday morning in September.
The attacks were by al-Qaeda terrorists.
clues.friday: Two planes hit the World Trade Center.

puzzles.context_card: On September 11, 2001, nearly 3,000 people were killed in a series of coordinated terrorist attacks. The FBI quickly opened an investigation called PENTTBOM. Within days, President George W. Bush had signed the USA PATRIOT Act. The attacks reshaped American foreign policy, airport security, and domestic surveillance in ways still felt today.

puzzles.headline: Beatlemania arrives in America and changes pop music forever

monday: A British Invasion began with this event.
The band appeared on a major American TV show shortly after arriving.
tuesday: They landed at a New York airport to screaming fans.
This occurred at the height of youth cultural revolution.
wednesday: This happened the same year a major civil rights act was signed.
The Fab Four's arrival triggered mass hysteria.
clues.friday: This year saw a British musical invasion.

puzzles.context_card: When The Beatles landed at JFK Airport on February 7, 1964, they were met by 3,000 screaming fans — the largest welcome in airport history to that point. They appeared on Ed Sullivan Show ten days later, watched by 73 million viewers. Within weeks, Beatlemania had completely reshaped American pop music and youth culture.

puzzles.headline: A computer company CEO introduces a device that puts the world in your pocket

monday: This was unveiled by the CEO of a computer company.
The announcement promised to revolutionize mobile phones.
tuesday: The year also saw the start of a global financial crisis.
The device had no physical keyboard.
wednesday: This was in the second decade of the 21st century.
The company was founded in a garage in California.
clues.thursday: This was a product announcement that changed an industry.
clues.friday: The device was a smartphone.
clues.saturday: It was unveiled by Steve Jobs.

puzzles.context_card: Steve Jobs unveiled the iPhone on January 9, 2007, calling it 'a revolutionary product.' It didn't go on sale until June, but it immediately changed what people expected from mobile devices. The initial model had no App Store — that came in 2008. By 2011, the iPhone had made Apple the most valuable company in the world.

puzzles.headline: The red flag over the Kremlin comes down, ending an era

monday: This happened during a period of rapid political change.
The superpower was led by Mikhail Gorbachev before this happened.
tuesday: The event happened around Christmas time in a specific year.
The country had been a superpower since after World War II.
wednesday: This occurred at the end of the Cold War.
The USSR was dissolved into 15 independent states.
clues.friday: Mikhail Gorbachev was a key figure.
clues.saturday: The Cold War ended with this event.

puzzles.context_card: On December 26, 1991, the Supreme Soviet of the USSR ratified the Belovezh Accords, formally ending the Soviet Union. What had been the world's largest country by area splintered into 15 independent nations. For 69 years, the Soviet experiment had shaped global politics. Its end brought both hope for a new world order and anxiety about nuclear weapons in unstable new states.

puzzles.headline: A computer engineer sends the first message over a network that would become the internet

monday: This happened at a US defense research institution.
The network would eventually evolve into the World Wide Web.
tuesday: The first message was sent between two computers in different locations.
This predates the personal computer revolution.
wednesday: This was in the early 1970s.
The sender worked on ARPANET, a precursor to the internet.
clues.thursday: This was at a defense research institution.
clues.friday: It was in the early 1970s.

puzzles.context_card: Ray Tomlinson sent the first email in 1971, choosing the @ symbol to separate the user name from the computer name. The message itself was largely forgettable — something like 'QWERTYUIOP.' But the @ sign became Tomlinson's lasting contribution to digital communication, now used in billions of email addresses worldwide.

puzzles.event: The Great Wall of China Completed
puzzles.event_formal: Completion of the Great Wall of China
puzzles.headline: After centuries of construction, the longest wall in the world is finished

monday: This is one of the most recognizable structures in human history.
It was built to protect against invasions from the north.
tuesday: Construction took place over many dynasties.
The wall is visible from space — a popular myth.
wednesday: This happened in the 17th century.
The Ming Dynasty had recently ended when it was completed.
clues.friday: It was built over many dynasties.
clues.saturday: It's visible from space — or so they say.

puzzles.context_card: The Great Wall was built, rebuilt, and maintained over centuries by millions of workers, many of whom died building it. The wall we see today was mostly built during the Ming Dynasty (1368-1644). The total length, including all branches, is over 13,000 miles. Contrary to popular myth, it's not visible from space with the naked eye.

puzzles.headline: Parisians storm a medieval fortress and spark a revolution

monday: This happened during the Enlightenment era.
A medieval fortress in Paris was attacked.
tuesday: The event happened in the summer.
It led to the end of absolute monarchy in France.
wednesday: This occurred in the late 18th century.
France was ruled by King Louis XVI at the time.
clues.friday: The Bastille was a prison in Paris.

puzzles.context_card: On July 14, 1789, several hundred Parisians stormed the Bastille, a medieval fortress that had become a symbol of royal tyranny. They freed the seven prisoners inside and killed the governor. The event became the defining moment of the French Revolution and is now commemorated annually as Bastille Day, France's national holiday.

puzzles.headline: The world's first nuclear explosion lights up the New Mexico desert

monday: This happened in the American Southwest.
The project was called the Manhattan Project.
tuesday: The test was conducted before the end of World War II.
Robert Oppenheimer was the scientific director.
wednesday: This happened in the 1940s.
The desert landscape glowed brighter than any light ever seen before.
clues.friday: It was in the 1940s.
clues.saturday: The desert lit up brighter than daylight.

puzzles.context_card: At 5:29 AM on July 16, 1945, J. Robert Oppenheimer watched the Trinity test explode with the words of the Bhagavad Gita in his mind: 'Now I am become Death, the destroyer of worlds.' The blast was visible 200 miles away. Three weeks later, atomic bombs destroyed Hiroshima and Nagasaki, ending World War II.

puzzles.event_formal: Theft of the Mona Lisa from the Louvre
puzzles.headline: The world's most famous painting vanishes from a Paris museum

monday: The theft made the painting more famous than ever.
The thief was a handyman who worked at the museum.
tuesday: The painting was missing for two years before being recovered.
The artist was an Italian Renaissance master.
wednesday: This happened in the 1910s.
The theft occurred in Paris.
clues.friday: The painting was missing for two years.

puzzles.context_card: Vincenzo Peruggia, a Louvre employee, stole the Mona Lisa by hiding in a broom closet overnight. He simply walked out with it under his coat the next morning. It was missing for two years and was found in Florence in 1913. Paradoxically, the theft transformed the Mona Lisa from a respected painting into a global celebrity.

puzzles.event_formal: Yuri Gagarin becomes first human in space
puzzles.headline: A Soviet cosmonaut becomes the first human to journey into outer space

monday: This was a major victory in the Space Race.
The cosmonaut was from the Soviet Union.
tuesday: His spacecraft made one orbit around Earth.
He said 'Earth is blue' when he saw it from space.
wednesday: This happened in the 1960s.
His flight lasted 108 minutes.
clues.friday: He was a Soviet cosmonaut.
clues.saturday: His famous words were about Earth being blue.

puzzles.context_card: Yuri Gagarin orbited Earth in Vostok 1 on April 12, 1961, completing one orbit in 108 minutes. At 27, he became the first human to see Earth from space. He parachuted from his capsule before it landed — ejection at 7km was standard procedure. A city in Kazakhstan was renamed Baikonur in his honor, and his face became a symbol of Soviet achievement.

puzzles.headline: Two Stanford students launch a search engine that would organize the world's information

monday: The company started in a garage — actually, a friend's garage.
The name comes from a misspelling.
tuesday: The founders were computer science PhD students.
The first investment was $100,000 from an angel investor.
wednesday: This happened in the late 1990s.
The company began in Menlo Park, California.
clues.friday: The founders were Stanford students.

puzzles.context_card: Larry Page and Sergey Brin met at Stanford in 1995 and initially didn't get along. Their search algorithm, PageRank, ranked websites by their links rather than keywords. Their first office was a garage rented from a friend. The name 'Google' came from 'googol,' a mathematical term for 1 followed by 100 zeros.

puzzles.event_formal: First recognition of AIDS as a new disease
puzzles.headline: Doctors notice a mysterious new illness affecting young gay men

monday: This was first reported in Los Angeles and New York.
Early cases appeared in otherwise healthy young men.
tuesday: The disease was initially called 'GRID' (Gay-Related Immune Deficiency).
Scientists later identified the virus causing it.
wednesday: This was in the early 1980s.
It would become one of the deadliest pandemics in history.
clues.friday: It was first noticed in Los Angeles.

puzzles.context_card: The CDC's Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report on June 5, 1981, documented five young gay men in Los Angeles with a rare pneumonia. By the end of 1981, there were 159 reported cases. The disease wouldn't be called AIDS until 1982, and the HIV virus wouldn't be identified until 1984. It would eventually kill 35 million people worldwide.

puzzles.headline: A former hedge fund analyst starts an online bookstore in his garage

monday: The founder started this in his garage in Seattle.
He originally named it Cadabra.com.
tuesday: The company went public three years after being founded.
Initially, the founder warned investors it would take 5-7 years to profit.
wednesday: This happened in the mid-1990s.
The name was changed because it was easier to remember.
clues.friday: The founder started in his garage.

puzzles.context_card: Jeff Bezos left his hedge fund job in 1994 to start an online bookstore, originally called Cadabra. He chose the name Amazon because it sounded 'exotic and different,' and the Amazon River was the biggest river he could think of. He raised $1 million from his own parents — the minimum viable investment to start. By 2020, Amazon had a market cap of $1.7 trillion.

puzzles.event_formal: Watson and Crick publish DNA double helix structure
puzzles.headline: Two scientists in Cambridge reveal the secret of life itself

monday: This discovery was made in England.
The scientists used X-ray crystallography data from Rosalind Franklin.
tuesday: The finding was published in the journal Nature.
It revolutionized biology and medicine.
wednesday: This was in the 1950s.
The discovery explained how genetic information is passed on.
clues.friday: The discovery was in England.

puzzles.context_card: Watson and Crick published their double helix model in Nature on April 25, 1953. Their famous line: 'It has not escaped our notice that the specific pairing we have postulated immediately suggests a possible copying mechanism for the genetic material.' Rosalind Franklin's Photo 51, which showed the helix structure, was critical — but she died in 1958 and never shared the Nobel Prize.

puzzles.headline: A reactor explodes at a Soviet nuclear power plant, releasing radioactive fallout

monday: This happened during a safety test at a Ukrainian power plant.
The Soviet government initially tried to cover up the disaster.
tuesday: The explosion sent a plume of radioactive material across Europe.
The town of Pripyat was evacuated.
wednesday: This occurred in the 1980s.
Ukraine was part of the Soviet Union at the time.
clues.friday: The town of Pripyat was evacuated.

puzzles.context_card: On April 26, 1986, Reactor 4 at Chernobyl exploded during a low-power test. The fire released radioactive material 9 kilometers into the atmosphere. Soviet authorities evacuated Pripyat (population 49,000) 36 hours later. The final death toll remains disputed — from 31 immediate deaths to estimates of thousands of cancer cases from radiation exposure. The destroyed reactor was finally enclosed in a giant steel sarcophagus in 2016.

puzzles.headline: A Korean company creates a device that would revolutionize how we listen to music

monday: This invention came from South Korea.
The technology compressed audio files without major quality loss.
tuesday: The first model held only about 12 songs.
It would take the iPod to make this technology mainstream.
wednesday: This was in the late 1990s.
It was developed by Saehan Information Systems.
clues.friday: It was from South Korea.

puzzles.context_card: The Saehan MP-X, released in 1997, was the first portable MP3 player. It held about 12 songs using flash memory and cost around $250. It had no screen and required a PC to load music. The technology was made mainstream by the iPod in 2001, which Apple co-founder Steve Jobs dismissed as 'just a feature' — meaning a flash-based MP3 player — before reconsidering.

puzzles.headline: A new currency begins a new era for European financial integration

monday: This currency replaced multiple national currencies in Europe.
It was first introduced electronically before physical notes appeared.
tuesday: Twelve European countries initially adopted it.
Germany, France, and Italy were among the original members.
wednesday: This happened at the turn of the millennium.
The currency was designed to unify European markets.
clues.thursday: This was at the turn of the millennium.
clues.friday: Twelve countries first adopted it.

puzzles.context_card: The Euro was introduced as an accounting currency on January 1, 1999, with physical notes and coins following on January 1, 2002. Twelve countries abandoned their national currencies — including the Deutsche Mark, French Franc, and Italian Lira — for the first time in history. The transition was initially chaotic in some countries, with ATM glitches and confusion at stores.

puzzles.headline: Two Stanford graduates launch a photo-sharing app that reaches 1 million users in two months

monday: The app was created by two people who sold it to Facebook for $1 billion.
The name combines 'instant camera' and 'telegram.'
tuesday: Only iPhone users could use it at first.
It popularized square photo filters.
wednesday: This was in the early 2010s.
It was acquired by Facebook just 18 months after launch.
clues.friday: It was founded by two people.

puzzles.context_card: Kevin Systrom and Mike Krieger launched Instagram on October 6, 2010. It reached 1 million users in just two months. Systrom had 12 employees when Facebook acquired it for $1 billion in 2012 — an astonishing valuation for a company with 13 employees and no revenue. By 2018, Instagram had 1 billion users and was Facebook's most valuable asset.

puzzles.headline: German scientists create a format that would kill the CD and change music forever

monday: This format compressed audio files by removing sounds humans can't hear.
The researchers worked at the Fraunhofer Institute in Germany.
tuesday: It made music piracy explode by enabling fast downloads.
Napster was built around this format.
wednesday: This was in the early 1990s.
It compressed audio roughly 10-12 times without major quality loss.
clues.friday: The researchers were from Germany.

puzzles.context_card: The MP3 format was developed by the Fraunhofer Institute in Germany and standardized in 1991. It could compress a 3.5-minute CD track from 30MB to about 3MB. The researchers initially struggled to find companies interested — nobody believed people would want CD-quality music compressed so much. They earned about $1 per licensed player. By 2000, MP3 had destroyed the CD singles market and would eventually help nearly destroy physical album sales.

puzzles.headline: The stock market collapses, ending the Roaring Twenties and triggering the Great Depression

monday: This happened in October.
Billions of dollars in stock value vanished in days.
tuesday: The crash ended a decade of reckless speculation.
It led to the Great Depression.
wednesday: This was in the 1920s.
It was the worst stock market crash in American history at the time.
clues.friday: It happened in October.

puzzles.context_card: On October 29, 1929, investors traded 16 million shares in a panic. The Dow fell 12% in a single day. Over the next few weeks, $30 billion in stock value evaporated — equivalent to about $400 billion today. The crash ended the Roaring Twenties and triggered the Great Depression, which would last a decade and see 25% unemployment in the United States.

puzzles.headline: A South African surgeon performs the first successful human-to-human heart transplant

monday: The surgeon was from South Africa.
The operation lasted about 5 hours.
tuesday: The recipient was a 53-year-old dentist.
The donor was a woman who died in a car accident.
wednesday: This was in the 1960s.
The surgeon was Dr. Christiaan Barnard.
clues.friday: The surgeon was from South Africa.

puzzles.context_card: On December 3, 1967, Dr. Christiaan Barnard transplanted a human heart into 53-year-old Louis Washkansky. The operation was successful, but Washkansky died 18 days later from pneumonia — his immune system had been weakened by the anti-rejection drugs. Barnard's face was on magazine covers worldwide overnight. He had gone from obscurity to celebrity in a single operation.

puzzles.event_formal: Salk polio vaccine declared safe and effective
puzzles.headline: A scientist announces a vaccine that would nearly eradicate a feared disease

monday: The vaccine was developed by Jonas Salk.
Parents feared this disease because it paralyzed children.
tuesday: The announcement was made on April 12 — a Monday.
Church bells rang across America when the news broke.
wednesday: This happened in the 1950s.
The disease especially affected children in summer.
clues.friday: Jonas Salk developed it.

puzzles.context_card: On April 12, 1955, Dr. Thomas Francis announced that Jonas Salk's polio vaccine was 'safe, effective, and potent.' Within hours, churches rang bells and factories blew whistles in celebration. One parent said: 'I have never experienced such a feeling of relief.' Within months, cases of polio in the US dropped by 90%. By 1979, polio was eliminated in the United States.

puzzles.headline: The first message travels between two computers on ARPANET

monday: The message was sent from Cambridge, Massachusetts.
Ray Tomlinson chose the @ symbol for email addresses.
tuesday: The exact content of the message has been lost.
It was sent over ARPANET, the precursor to the internet.
wednesday: This was in the early 1970s.
The sender was a computer programmer.
clues.friday: Ray Tomlinson sent it.

puzzles.context_card: Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email between two computers on ARPANET in 1971. The message itself was mundane — probably 'QWERTYUIOP' or something similar, used as a keyboard test. Tomlinson also chose the @ sign to separate user names from computer names, creating the format we still use today. The invention went from concept to global essential service in under three decades.

puzzles.event_formal: Napoleon Bonaparte crowned Emperor of France
puzzles.headline: A former revolutionary crowns himself Emperor in a ceremony at Notre-Dame

monday: The coronation took place at Notre-Dame Cathedral in Paris.
Napoleon famously took the crown from the Pope and placed it on his own head.
tuesday: The ceremony was painted by Jacques-Louis David.
This came after the French Revolution overthrew the monarchy.
wednesday: This was in the early 19th century.
The Pope was present but played a passive role.
clues.thursday: This was in the early 19th century.
clues.friday: The ceremony was at Notre-Dame.

puzzles.context_card: On December 2, 1804, Napoleon Bonaparte crowned himself Emperor of France in Notre-Dame. He famously seized the crown from Pope Pius VII and placed it on his own head before crowning his wife Josephine. The painter Jacques-Louis David immortalized the moment in a massive canvas. Napoleon's empire would eventually span most of Europe before his defeat at Waterloo in 1815.

puzzles.headline: Three former PayPal employees launch a video-sharing website that would change media forever

monday: The first video was uploaded by a co-founder.
The site was sold to Google 18 months after launch.
tuesday: The original name consideration was 'Tune In, Hook Up.'
The first video was called 'Me at the zoo.'
wednesday: This was in the mid-2000s.
It transformed how media is created and consumed.
clues.friday: The first video was 'Me at the zoo.'

puzzles.context_card: YouTube was founded in February 2005 and launched in November. The first video, 'Me at the zoo,' was uploaded by co-founder Jawed Karim on April 23, 2005, and is still online. Google acquired YouTube in October 2006 for $1.65 billion — at the time, an enormous sum for a company with no revenue and 25 employees. By 2023, YouTube had over 2 billion logged-in monthly users.
